lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Alack, alack, Edmund, I like not this unnatural dealing. When I desire

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: their leave that I might pity him, they took from me the

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: use of mine own house; charged me, on pain of their perpetual

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: displeasure, neither to speak of him, entreat for him, nor any way

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: sustain him.

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: Most savage and unnatural!

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Go to; say you nothing. There’s a division betwixt the dukes; and

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: a worse matter than that: I have received a letter this night;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: ’tis dangerous to be spoken; I have locked the letter in my

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: closet: these injuries the king now bears will be revenged home; there’s

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: part of a power already footed: we must incline to the king.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I will seek him, and privily relieve him: go you and maintain

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: talk with the duke, that my charity be not of him perceived:

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: if he ask for me. I am ill, and gone to bed.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Though I die for it, as no less is threatened me, the

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: king my old master must be relieved. There is some strange thing

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: toward, Edmund; pray you, be careful.

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: This courtesy, forbid thee, shall the duke

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: Instantly know; and of that letter too:

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: This seems a fair deserving, and must draw me

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: That which my father loses; no less than all:

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: The younger rises when the old doth fall.

glosses.definition: legitimate merit — Edmund's aside on his own advancement through betrayal
Gloucester's veiled reference to the French invasion

glosses.phrase: These injuries the king now bears
glosses.definition: Lear's present sufferings, which Gloucester intends to relieve
privately — Gloucester's secret succor to Lear

annotations.title: Gloucester's mistake — Edmund becomes an informer
annotations.body: Gloucester tells Edmund, in confidence, that he knows of a secret letter and intends to help Lear against the sisters' wishes. He even names the forbidden 'pity' he feels. Edmund's aside — 'This courtesy, forbid thee, shall the duke / Instantly know' — seals Gloucester's fate. The scene is twenty-two lines long and contains the whole mechanism by which Gloucester will be blinded. Shakespeare shows how every tragic sequence in this play runs on premature confidence: a parent telling a child a secret the child will weaponise. The structural echo of Lear's premature gift of the kingdom in 1.1 is exact.

annotations.title: Gloucester's 'strange news' — pity in a time when pity is forbidden
annotations.body: Gloucester tells Edmund he has had news of the impending French invasion and intends to aid Lear — despite Cornwall's prohibition. 'There is some strange thing toward, Edmund; pray you, be careful.' That Gloucester confides this to Edmund completes the parallel with Lear confiding in Goneril and Regan: each father hands his fatal intelligence to the child who will destroy him. But notice the play's specific moral move: Gloucester does this because he has chosen to risk himself for Lear. His first act of sustained moral courage is the act that dooms him. The play makes a dark claim here — in a sufficiently corrupt order, virtue is self-destructive. Gloucester will be blinded precisely because he tried to behave well.

trials.prompt: How does 3.3 parallel the opening scene?
options: A parent (Gloucester) confides in a child (Edmund) who instantly betrays him — echoing Lear's premature gift of trust to his daughters in 1.1
It is set in the same location as 1.1
It takes place at the same time of day

trials.prompt: What claim does the play make through Gloucester's fate — blinded for trying to help Lear?
options: A dark moral: in a sufficiently corrupt order, virtue is self-destructive; Gloucester's first act of sustained moral courage is what dooms him
